questions.question: Approximate $\int_{0}^{3} \frac{x}{x^2 + 1} \, dx$ using a right Riemann sum with 6 subintervals.
Use a left Riemann sum with $n = 4$ to estimate $\int_{0}^{2} \sqrt{4 - x^2} \, dx$ (quarter circle area).

The table gives values of $f(x)$ at irregular intervals. Use a left Riemann sum to approximate $\int_{0}^{12} f(x) \, dx$.

$\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c} x & 0 & 3 & 5 & 9 & 12 \\ \hline f(x) & 2 & 5 & 7 & 3 & 4 \end{array}$
Approximate $\int_{0}^{2} \sin(x^2) \, dx$ using a midpoint Riemann sum with 4 subintervals.
The rate of energy consumption (MW) is $r(t) = 100 + 50\cos(\pi t/6)$. Use a right Riemann sum with 6 subintervals to estimate the total energy used from $t = 0$ to $t = 12$ hours.
For $f(x) = e^{-x^2}$ on $[0,2]$, use a midpoint Riemann sum with 5 subintervals.

Given the table with unequal spacing, use a midpoint sum (with linear interpolation for midpoint values) to approximate $\int_{0}^{10} g(x) \, dx$.

$\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c} x & 0 & 2 & 5 & 8 & 10 \\ \hline g(x) & 3 & 7 & 4 & 6 & 2 \end{array}$
questions.explanation: Subintervals: [0,2], [2,5], [5,8], [8,10] with widths 2, 3, 3, 2. Midpoints: 1, 3.5, 6.5, 9. Interpolated values: $g(1) \approx 5$, $g(3.5) \approx 5.5$, $g(6.5) \approx 5$, $g(9) \approx 4$. Sum = $5\times2 + 5.5\times3 + 5\times3 + 4\times2 = 10 + 16.5 + 15 + 8 = 49.5$.

questions.question: Approximate $\int_{0}^{\pi} x\sin x \, dx$ using a left Riemann sum with $n = 4$.
A velocity function is given by $v(t) = 4t - t^2$. Use a midpoint sum with 8 subintervals to estimate displacement from $t = 0$ to $t = 4$.
Which of the following represents a right Riemann sum with $n = 12$ to approximate $\int_{1}^{4} \ln(1 + x^2) \, dx$?

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered challenging Riemann sum applications.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! You understand Riemann sums well. Review irregular interval calculations.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on calculating $\Delta x$ and identifying correct endpoints.
scoreMessages.0: Review the definition of Riemann sums: $\sum f(x_i^*) \Delta x$.
